        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm looking to improve my marketing strategy for my business. I've been trying to implement some new techniques I learned from a digital marketing workshop I attended recently. By the way, I've been quite active in attending workshops lately - I've attended five in the last three months.
assistant: That's great to hear! It's excellent that you're taking proactive steps to improve your marketing strategy and investing time in attending workshops to learn new techniques. Five workshops in three months is impressive - you're definitely committed to upskilling and staying up-to-date with the latest trends and best practices.

What specific areas of marketing are you looking to improve or focus on? Are you looking to increase brand awareness, generate more leads, or drive conversions? Have you identified any gaps in your current strategy that you're hoping to address with the new techniques you've learned?

Also, what kind of feedback or insights did you gain from the workshops that you're looking to implement in your business? Were there any particular topics or speakers that resonated with you, or any "aha" moments that you're excited to put into practice?
user: I'm looking to focus on social media advertising, especially Facebook Ads, as I think it's a key area where I can reach more customers. I've been trying to optimize my ad campaigns using some tips I learned from the digital marketing workshop, but I'm not sure if I'm doing it right. I'd like to get some guidance on how to set up my ad targeting and bidding strategy to get the best ROI.
assistant: Facebook Ads can be a powerful way to reach your target audience and drive results for your business.

Optimizing ad campaigns can be a bit tricky, but don't worry, I'm here to help. Let's break down the key areas you'll want to focus on to get the best ROI from your Facebook Ads:

1. **Targeting**: Who are your ideal customers? What are their interests, behaviors, and demographics? Facebook offers a range of targeting options, including:
	* Demographics: age, gender, location, language, etc.
	* Interests: hobbies, pages liked, etc.
	* Behaviors: purchase behavior, job title, etc.
	* Custom Audiences: targeting people who have engaged with your business or similar audiences
2. **Ad Creative**: What makes your ad stand out? You'll want to create eye-catching visuals and compelling copy that resonates with your target audience. Consider:
	* Image or video: high-quality visuals that grab attention
	* Headline: clear, concise, and relevant
	* Text: concise, informative, and persuasive
	* Call-to-Action (CTA): clear and prominent
3. **Bidding Strategy**: How do you want to optimize your ad spend? Facebook offers various bidding strategies, including:
	* Cost per Click (CPC): bid for each ad click
	* Cost per Thousand Impressions (CPM): bid for every 1,000 ad impressions
	* Cost per Action (CPA): bid for specific actions, like conversions or leads
	* Optimized Cost per Click (oCPC): Facebook optimizes for CPC based on your target cost per action
4. **Budget and Pacing**: How much are you willing to spend, and how do you want to allocate your budget? Consider:
	* Daily budget: set a daily limit for your ad spend
	* Lifetime budget: set a total budget for your ad campaign
	* Pacing: control how quickly your budget is spent throughout the day

To get started, can you tell me a bit more about your current ad campaigns? What are your goals, and what kind of ad creative are you using? What's your current targeting strategy, and how are you bidding on your ads?

Also, what specific tips did you learn from the workshop that you're trying to implement, and where are you getting stuck? I'm here to help you troubleshoot and provide guidance to optimize your Facebook Ads for the best ROI.
user: I'm currently running a campaign targeting people interested in photography and entrepreneurship, as those are the main themes of my business. I've set a daily budget of $20, and I'm using a cost per click bidding strategy. I've been trying to optimize my ad creative by using high-quality images and clear headlines, but I'm not sure if my targeting is specific enough. I learned from the workshop that it's important to have a clear target audience in mind, but I'm not sure how to narrow it down further. I've also been trying to use some of the SEO tips I learned to improve my website's visibility, but I'm not sure if it's helping my Facebook Ads performance.
assistant: It sounds like you're on the right track, but there are a few areas where you can refine your targeting and strategy to improve your Facebook Ads performance.

**Targeting**:

* Interests: "photography" and "entrepreneurship" are quite broad interests. Consider narrowing them down to more specific sub-niches, such as:
	+ Photography: portrait, landscape, wildlife, wedding, etc.
	+ Entrepreneurship: startup founders, small business owners, freelancers, etc.
* Additional targeting options:
	+ Behaviors: people who have shown interest in photography or entrepreneurship-related products or services
	+ Job titles: photographers, entrepreneurs, creatives, etc.
	+ Education: people with a degree in a related field or those who have taken online courses
	+ Income: target people with a certain income level or those who have shown purchasing power
* Exclusions: exclude people who are not your ideal audience, such as students or people outside your target location

**Ad Creative**:

* High-quality images are a must, but make sure they're relevant to your target audience and ad message.
* Clear headlines are important, but also consider using attention-grabbing headlines that resonate with your target audience.
* Consider using social proof, such as customer testimonials or reviews, to build credibility and trust.

**Bidding Strategy**:

* Cost per click (CPC) can be effective, but consider testing other bidding strategies, such as cost per action (CPA) or optimized cost per click (oCPC), to optimize for specific actions or conversions.
* Set up conversion tracking to measure the effectiveness of your ads and optimize for better ROI.

**SEO and Facebook Ads**:

* While SEO is important for your website's visibility, it's a separate channel from Facebook Ads. Focus on optimizing your Facebook Ads for Facebook's algorithm and ad auction, rather than relying on SEO tips.
* However, if you're driving traffic to your website from Facebook Ads, make sure your website is optimized for conversions, with clear calls-to-action and a seamless user experience.

**Next Steps**:

1. Refine your targeting by adding more specific interests, behaviors, and demographics to your ad sets.
2. Test different ad creatives, including images, headlines, and copy, to see what resonates best with your target audience.
3. Consider setting up conversion tracking and optimizing for CPA or oCPC bidding strategies.
4. Monitor your ad performance closely and make adjustments based on the data.

How do these suggestions sound? Are there any specific areas you'd like to focus on or questions you have about implementing these changes?
user: I think I need to revisit my targeting strategy. I'll definitely try to narrow down my target audience and add some more specific interests and behaviors. I'll also experiment with different ad creatives to see what resonates best with my audience. By the way, I've been trying to implement some SEO techniques I learned from the digital marketing workshop, but I'm not sure if I'm doing it right. Do you have any resources or tips on how to optimize my website for search engines?
assistant: Revisiting your targeting strategy and ad creatives will definitely help improve your Facebook Ads performance.

Regarding SEO, I'd be happy to help! Optimizing your website for search engines can be a bit complex, but here are some actionable tips to get you started:

**Keyword Research**:

1. Identify your target keywords: Brainstorm keywords related to your business, such as "photography tips," "entrepreneurship resources," or "small business marketing."
2. Use keyword research tools: Tools like Google Keyword Planner, Ahrefs, or SEMrush can help you find relevant keywords and their search volume.
3. Analyze competitors: Research your competitors' websites and identify gaps in their keyword strategy.

**On-Page Optimization**:

1. **Title Tags**: Write unique, descriptive, and keyword-rich title tags for each page (<60 characters).
2. **Meta Descriptions**: Craft compelling and informative meta descriptions for each page (<160 characters).
3. **Header Tags**: Organize your content with H1, H2, H3, etc. header tags.
4. **Content**: Write high-quality, engaging, and keyword-rich content (>500 words).
5. **URL Structure**: Use descriptive and keyword-rich URLs for each page.
6. **Image Optimization**: Optimize image file names, alt tags, and descriptions with target keywords.

**Technical Optimization**:

1. **Page Speed**: Ensure your website loads quickly (<3 seconds) using tools like Google PageSpeed Insights.
2. **Mobile-Friendliness**: Ensure your website is responsive and mobile-friendly.
3. **SSL Certificate**: Install an SSL certificate to secure your website (HTTPS).
4. **XML Sitemap**: Create and submit a sitemap to help search engines crawl your website.
5. **Robots.txt**: Use a robots.txt file to control how search engines crawl and index your website.

**Link Building**:

1. **Internal Linking**: Create a clear hierarchy and link to relevant pages on your website.
2. **External Linking**: Link to high-quality, relevant external resources to add credibility.
3. **Guest Blogging**: Write guest posts for other reputable websites to build backlinks to your website.
4. **Broken Link Building**: Find and replace broken links on other websites with links to your own relevant content.

**Tracking and Analysis**:

1. **Google Analytics**: Set up and track website analytics to monitor traffic, engagement, and conversion rates.
2. **Google Search Console**: Monitor search engine impressions, clicks, and rankings.

These are just some of the basics to get you started. Remember, SEO is an ongoing process that requires regular optimization and improvement.

For more resources, I recommend checking out:

* Moz's Beginner's Guide to SEO
* Ahrefs' SEO Guide
* Google's SEO Starter Guide

Do you have any specific SEO questions or areas you'd like to focus on?
user: That's a lot of helpful information! I think I need to work on my keyword research and on-page optimization. I'll make sure to check out those resources you mentioned. By the way, I mentioned earlier that I've been attending a lot of workshops lately - I've actually attended five in the last three months, and I've been finding them really helpful for my business.
assistant: It's great that you're taking proactive steps to improve your business, and attending workshops is an excellent way to learn from experts and network with like-minded individuals. Five workshops in three months is impressive - you're definitely committed to continuous learning and growth!

Which workshops have you found most helpful, and what specific insights or takeaways have you applied to your business? Are there any particular areas you're looking to focus on in the next few months, such as marketing, sales, or operations?

Remember, it's essential to take action on what you've learned and implement changes to see real results. Don't be afraid to experiment, try new things, and adjust your strategy as needed. And don't hesitate to reach out if you need guidance or have questions - that's what I'm here for!

Keep up the great work, and I'm excited to hear about your progress!
user: I think I need to clarify that I attended five workshops in the last three months, with the most recent one being a digital marketing workshop, and I've been trying to implement some of the techniques I learned from it. I've also been trying to attend at least one workshop a month to learn new skills and network with people in different industries.
assistant: It's clear now that you've been attending a series of workshops, with the most recent one being a digital marketing workshop, and you're actively trying to implement the techniques you learned from it.

That's a great approach to continuous learning and professional development! Attending workshops regularly can help you stay up-to-date with the latest trends and best practices, network with people from different backgrounds, and gain new insights to apply to your business.

It's impressive that you've committed to attending at least one workshop a month. That shows your dedication to improving your skills and growing your business.

How do you find the networking aspect of workshops? Have you met anyone interesting or made any valuable connections at the workshops you've attended?

Also, what specific techniques from the digital marketing workshop are you trying to implement, and how's the progress going? Do you have any questions or need help with any of the techniques?

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles

# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

- exploratory conversation

        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm looking to improve my marketing strategy for my business. I've been trying to implement some new techniques I learned from a digital marketing workshop I attended recently. By the way, I've been quite active in attending workshops lately - I've attended five in the last three months.
assistant: That's great to hear! It's excellent that you're taking proactive steps to improve your marketing strategy and investing time in attending workshops to learn new techniques. Five workshops in three months is impressive - you're definitely committed to upskilling and staying up-to-date with the latest trends and best practices.

What specific areas of marketing are you looking to improve or focus on? Are you looking to increase brand awareness, generate more leads, or drive conversions? Have you identified any gaps in your current strategy that you're hoping to address with the new techniques you've learned?

Also, what kind of feedback or insights did you gain from the workshops that you're looking to implement in your business? Were there any particular topics or speakers that resonated with you, or any "aha" moments that you're excited to put into practice?
user: I'm looking to focus on social media advertising, especially Facebook Ads, as I think it's a key area where I can reach more customers. I've been trying to optimize my ad campaigns using some tips I learned from the digital marketing workshop, but I'm not sure if I'm doing it right. I'd like to get some guidance on how to set up my ad targeting and bidding strategy to get the best ROI.
assistant: Facebook Ads can be a powerful way to reach your target audience and drive results for your business.

Optimizing ad campaigns can be a bit tricky, but don't worry, I'm here to help. Let's break down the key areas you'll want to focus on to get the best ROI from your Facebook Ads:

1. **Targeting**: Who are your ideal customers? What are their interests, behaviors, and demographics? Facebook offers a range of targeting options, including:
	* Demographics: age, gender, location, language, etc.
	* Interests: hobbies, pages liked, etc.
	* Behaviors: purchase behavior, job title, etc.
	* Custom Audiences: targeting people who have engaged with your business or similar audiences
2. **Ad Creative**: What makes your ad stand out? You'll want to create eye-catching visuals and compelling copy that resonates with your target audience. Consider:
	* Image or video: high-quality visuals that grab attention
	* Headline: clear, concise, and relevant
	* Text: concise, informative, and persuasive
	* Call-to-Action (CTA): clear and prominent
3. **Bidding Strategy**: How do you want to optimize your ad spend? Facebook offers various bidding strategies, including:
	* Cost per Click (CPC): bid for each ad click
	* Cost per Thousand Impressions (CPM): bid for every 1,000 ad impressions
	* Cost per Action (CPA): bid for specific actions, like conversions or leads
	* Optimized Cost per Click (oCPC): Facebook optimizes for CPC based on your target cost per action
4. **Budget and Pacing**: How much are you willing to spend, and how do you want to allocate your budget? Consider:
	* Daily budget: set a daily limit for your ad spend
	* Lifetime budget: set a total budget for your ad campaign
	* Pacing: control how quickly your budget is spent throughout the day

To get started, can you tell me a bit more about your current ad campaigns? What are your goals, and what kind of ad creative are you using? What's your current targeting strategy, and how are you bidding on your ads?

Also, what specific tips did you learn from the workshop that you're trying to implement, and where are you getting stuck? I'm here to help you troubleshoot and provide guidance to optimize your Facebook Ads for the best ROI.
user: I'm currently running a campaign targeting people interested in photography and entrepreneurship, as those are the main themes of my business. I've set a daily budget of $20, and I'm using a cost per click bidding strategy. I've been trying to optimize my ad creative by using high-quality images and clear headlines, but I'm not sure if my targeting is specific enough. I learned from the workshop that it's important to have a clear target audience in mind, but I'm not sure how to narrow it down further. I've also been trying to use some of the SEO tips I learned to improve my website's visibility, but I'm not sure if it's helping my Facebook Ads performance.
assistant: It sounds like you're on the right track, but there are a few areas where you can refine your targeting and strategy to improve your Facebook Ads performance.

**Targeting**:

* Interests: "photography" and "entrepreneurship" are quite broad interests. Consider narrowing them down to more specific sub-niches, such as:
	+ Photography: portrait, landscape, wildlife, wedding, etc.
	+ Entrepreneurship: startup founders, small business owners, freelancers, etc.
* Additional targeting options:
	+ Behaviors: people who have shown interest in photography or entrepreneurship-related products or services
	+ Job titles: photographers, entrepreneurs, creatives, etc.
	+ Education: people with a degree in a related field or those who have taken online courses
	+ Income: target people with a certain income level or those who have shown purchasing power
* Exclusions: exclude people who are not your ideal audience, such as students or people outside your target location

**Ad Creative**:

* High-quality images are a must, but make sure they're relevant to your target audience and ad message.
* Clear headlines are important, but also consider using attention-grabbing headlines that resonate with your target audience.
* Consider using social proof, such as customer testimonials or reviews, to build credibility and trust.

**Bidding Strategy**:

* Cost per click (CPC) can be effective, but consider testing other bidding strategies, such as cost per action (CPA) or optimized cost per click (oCPC), to optimize for specific actions or conversions.
* Set up conversion tracking to measure the effectiveness of your ads and optimize for better ROI.

**SEO and Facebook Ads**:

* While SEO is important for your website's visibility, it's a separate channel from Facebook Ads. Focus on optimizing your Facebook Ads for Facebook's algorithm and ad auction, rather than relying on SEO tips.
* However, if you're driving traffic to your website from Facebook Ads, make sure your website is optimized for conversions, with clear calls-to-action and a seamless user experience.

**Next Steps**:

1. Refine your targeting by adding more specific interests, behaviors, and demographics to your ad sets.
2. Test different ad creatives, including images, headlines, and copy, to see what resonates best with your target audience.
3. Consider setting up conversion tracking and optimizing for CPA or oCPC bidding strategies.
4. Monitor your ad performance closely and make adjustments based on the data.

How do these suggestions sound? Are there any specific areas you'd like to focus on or questions you have about implementing these changes?
user: I think I need to revisit my targeting strategy. I'll definitely try to narrow down my target audience and add some more specific interests and behaviors. I'll also experiment with different ad creatives to see what resonates best with my audience. By the way, I've been trying to implement some SEO techniques I learned from the digital marketing workshop, but I'm not sure if I'm doing it right. Do you have any resources or tips on how to optimize my website for search engines?
assistant: Revisiting your targeting strategy and ad creatives will definitely help improve your Facebook Ads performance.

Regarding SEO, I'd be happy to help! Optimizing your website for search engines can be a bit complex, but here are some actionable tips to get you started:

**Keyword Research**:

1. Identify your target keywords: Brainstorm keywords related to your business, such as "photography tips," "entrepreneurship resources," or "small business marketing."
2. Use keyword research tools: Tools like Google Keyword Planner, Ahrefs, or SEMrush can help you find relevant keywords and their search volume.
3. Analyze competitors: Research your competitors' websites and identify gaps in their keyword strategy.

**On-Page Optimization**:

1. **Title Tags**: Write unique, descriptive, and keyword-rich title tags for each page (<60 characters).
2. **Meta Descriptions**: Craft compelling and informative meta descriptions for each page (<160 characters).
3. **Header Tags**: Organize your content with H1, H2, H3, etc. header tags.
4. **Content**: Write high-quality, engaging, and keyword-rich content (>500 words).
5. **URL Structure**: Use descriptive and keyword-rich URLs for each page.
6. **Image Optimization**: Optimize image file names, alt tags, and descriptions with target keywords.

**Technical Optimization**:

1. **Page Speed**: Ensure your website loads quickly (<3 seconds) using tools like Google PageSpeed Insights.
2. **Mobile-Friendliness**: Ensure your website is responsive and mobile-friendly.
3. **SSL Certificate**: Install an SSL certificate to secure your website (HTTPS).
4. **XML Sitemap**: Create and submit a sitemap to help search engines crawl your website.
5. **Robots.txt**: Use a robots.txt file to control how search engines crawl and index your website.

**Link Building**:

1. **Internal Linking**: Create a clear hierarchy and link to relevant pages on your website.
2. **External Linking**: Link to high-quality, relevant external resources to add credibility.
3. **Guest Blogging**: Write guest posts for other reputable websites to build backlinks to your website.
4. **Broken Link Building**: Find and replace broken links on other websites with links to your own relevant content.

**Tracking and Analysis**:

1. **Google Analytics**: Set up and track website analytics to monitor traffic, engagement, and conversion rates.
2. **Google Search Console**: Monitor search engine impressions, clicks, and rankings.

These are just some of the basics to get you started. Remember, SEO is an ongoing process that requires regular optimization and improvement.

For more resources, I recommend checking out:

* Moz's Beginner's Guide to SEO
* Ahrefs' SEO Guide
* Google's SEO Starter Guide

Do you have any specific SEO questions or areas you'd like to focus on?
user: That's a lot of helpful information! I think I need to work on my keyword research and on-page optimization. I'll make sure to check out those resources you mentioned. By the way, I mentioned earlier that I've been attending a lot of workshops lately - I've actually attended five in the last three months, and I've been finding them really helpful for my business.
assistant: It's great that you're taking proactive steps to improve your business, and attending workshops is an excellent way to learn from experts and network with like-minded individuals. Five workshops in three months is impressive - you're definitely committed to continuous learning and growth!

Which workshops have you found most helpful, and what specific insights or takeaways have you applied to your business? Are there any particular areas you're looking to focus on in the next few months, such as marketing, sales, or operations?

Remember, it's essential to take action on what you've learned and implement changes to see real results. Don't be afraid to experiment, try new things, and adjust your strategy as needed. And don't hesitate to reach out if you need guidance or have questions - that's what I'm here for!

Keep up the great work, and I'm excited to hear about your progress!

I've also been trying to attend at least one workshop a month to learn new skills and network with people in different industries.

That's a great approach to continuous learning and professional development! Attending workshops regularly can help you stay up-to-date with the latest trends and best practices, network with people from different backgrounds, and gain new insights to apply to your business.

It's impressive that you've committed to attending at least one workshop a month. That shows your dedication to improving your skills and growing your business.
